text:
NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING REPORT
FoundationOne CDx (Comprehensive Genomic Profiling)
Tissue: Prostate Adenocarcinoma — Primary Diagnostic Biopsy
Concurrent Germline Analysis from Peripheral Blood

================================================================================
SPECIMEN INFORMATION
================================================================================
Patient Name: Raymond Kowalski
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Male
Ordering Physician: [Attending Oncologist, Genitourinary Oncology]
Specimen ID: FMI-2024-PRO-XXXXX
Tissue Source: Transrectal ultrasound (TRUS)-guided prostate biopsy, 12-core
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue cores (cores 3, 7, and 11 submitted for molecular analysis)
Percent Tumor Nuclei: 70%
Tumor Histology: Acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5
Germline Specimen: Peripheral blood (EDTA tube) — simultaneous submission for paired germline sequencing
Laboratory: Foundation Medicine, Inc., Cambridge, MA (CLIA Certified, CAP Accredited)

================================================================================
CLINICAL INDICATION
================================================================================
Metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma, newly diagnosed, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, cT3b N1 M1b. PSA at diagnosis 187 ng/mL. Extensive bone and pelvic lymph node metastases at presentation. Comprehensive genomic profiling requested to identify targetable alterations, prognostic biomarkers, and germline predisposition variants, including HRR gene status relevant to PARP inhibitor eligibility.

================================================================================
METHODS
================================================================================
FoundationOne CDx assay: hybrid-capture based comprehensive genomic profiling covering the entire coding sequence of 324 cancer-relevant genes plus select introns for fusion detection. Analysis includes substitutions, insertions/deletions, copy number alterations, and genomic rearrangements. Microsatellite instability (MSI) and tumor mutational burden (TMB) are reported. Germline testing was performed on matched peripheral blood DNA using a separate validated germline panel (FoundationOne Germline) covering known cancer predisposition genes including BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, CDH1, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, TP53, APC, MUTYH, and PTEN. Variant interpretation performed in accordance with AMP/ACMG classification guidelines.

================================================================================
SOURC OF VARIANTS REPORTED
================================================================================
All variants are reported from tumor tissue unless otherwise noted. Allele frequencies (VAF) are reported from tumor DNA. Germline status for selected variants confirmed by parallel blood-derived DNA analysis.

================================================================================
GENOMIC FINDINGS — TUMOR (Somatic and Germline-derived)
================================================================================

SHORT VARIANTS (Substitutions and Small Indels)
-----------------------------------------------

1. BRCA2 p.E1308* (c.3921G>A) — PATHOGENIC
   - Variant type: Nonsense (stop-gain)
   - Variant allele frequency (VAF): 48%
   - Classification: Pathogenic — associated with loss of BRCA2 tumor suppressor function
   - Germline status: CONFIRMED GERMLINE PATHOGENIC VARIANT (see Germline Section below)
   - Loss of heterozygosity (LOH): Present — tumor shows biallelic inactivation (germline mutation + somatic LOH)
   - Clinical significance: BRCA2 biallelic loss is a clinically actionable alteration in prostate cancer. Associated with aggressive disease biology, sensitivity to DNA-damaging agents, and eligibility for PARP inhibitor therapy (olaparib, rucaparib). Relevant to platinum chemotherapy sensitivity.

2. TP53 p.R248Q (c.743G>A) — PATHOGENIC
   - Variant type: Missense (gain-of-function/dominant negative)
   - VAF: 52%
   - Classification: Pathogenic — hotspot mutation at TP53 codon 248, among the most frequently mutated residues in human cancer; dominant-negative and gain-of-function properties described
   - Germline status: NOT detected in matched peripheral blood — somatic in origin
   - Clinical significance: TP53 mutation in prostate cancer is associated with aggressive disease, castration resistance, and risk of neuroendocrine transdifferentiation under treatment selective pressure. Concurrent with PTEN loss, indicative of high genomic instability.

3. CDK12 p.L786fs*2 (c.2355_2356del) — PATHOGENIC
   - Variant type: Frameshift deletion (loss of function)
   - VAF: 31%
   - Classification: Pathogenic — frameshift leading to premature stop codon
   - Germline status: NOT detected in matched peripheral blood — somatic in origin
   - Clinical significance: CDK12 loss-of-function mutations define a distinct genomic subtype of prostate cancer ("CDK12 mutant") associated with focal tandem duplications, elevated genomic instability, and possible immunotherapy responsiveness in some contexts. CDK12-mutant tumors may have reduced response to PARP inhibitors compared to BRCA2-mutant tumors, though in the context of concurrent BRCA2 biallelic loss, PARP inhibitor eligibility remains appropriate.

4. FOXA1 p.P358L — LIKELY PATHOGENIC
   - Variant type: Missense
   - VAF: 28%
   - Classification: Likely pathogenic — recurrent hotspot mutation in prostate cancer affecting the forkhead domain of FOXA1, a pioneer transcription factor involved in AR chromatin accessibility
   - Germline status: NOT detected in matched peripheral blood — somatic in origin
   - Clinical significance: FOXA1 mutations in the forkhead domain are enriched in high-grade and metastatic prostate cancers. Associated with altered AR transcriptional activity. Not directly targetable but informative for disease biology.

5. SPOP p.F133V — LIKELY PATHOGENIC
   - Variant type: Missense
   - VAF: 14% (subclonal)
   - Classification: Likely pathogenic — recurrent mutation at the MATH domain of SPOP; a canonical substrate-binding domain hotspot in prostate cancer
   - Germline status: NOT detected in matched peripheral blood — somatic in origin
   - Clinical significance: SPOP mutations are among the most common recurrent mutations in localized prostate cancer and define a distinct molecular subtype (SPOP-mutant class, typically ERG-negative, as confirmed in this specimen). Subclonal VAF (14%) suggests this is a secondary/subclonal event. SPOP mutations may confer sensitivity to BET bromodomain inhibitors (investigational). SPOP mutations may be associated with improved response to ADT-based regimens in some contexts.

NO ADDITIONAL PATHOGENIC OR LIKELY PATHOGENIC SHORT VARIANTS DETECTED IN:
   AR (exons 1–8, ligand-binding domain) — WILD-TYPE; no AR ligand-binding domain mutations detected at this time
   PIK3CA — wild-type
   KRAS, NRAS, HRAS — wild-type
   APC — wild-type
   CTNNB1 — wild-type
   IDH1/IDH2 — wild-type
   BRAF — wild-type
   MET — wild-type
   FGFR1/2/3/4 — wild-type
   ATM — wild-type
   PALB2 — wild-type
   BRCA1 — wild-type

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. PTEN — HOMOZYGOUS DELETION (exons 1–9, whole-gene loss)
   - Classification: Pathogenic — biallelic loss of PTEN tumor suppressor
   - Correlation: Consistent with IHC finding of heterogeneous PTEN loss on diagnostic biopsy
   - Clinical significance: PTEN deletion activates PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling; co-occurrence with BRCA2 loss represents a high-risk genomic phenotype in prostate cancer. Associated with poor prognosis, accelerated castration resistance, and possible synergy with PI3K pathway inhibitors (investigational, e.g., idelalisib, copanlisib; ipatasertib-based combinations in clinical trials).

2. RB1 — FOCAL DELETION (hemizygous loss; biallelic status indeterminate)
   - Classification: Pathogenic — RB1 loss of function
   - Clinical significance: RB1 deletion is a key driver of treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC). In combination with TP53 mutation (present here), biallelic RB1/TP53 loss is the canonical genomic signature of neuroendocrine transdifferentiation. Monitoring for neuroendocrine features (clinical, biochemical, and histologic) is warranted, particularly during and after hormonal therapy.

3. BRCA2 — COPY LOSS (consistent with LOH at mutant allele retained; see BRCA2 p.E1308* above)
   - Supports biallelic inactivation in conjunction with germline nonsense mutation.

4. MYC (8q24) — GAIN (low-level amplification)
   - Copy number: approximately 4–5 copies (gain above diploid baseline)
   - Clinical significance: MYC gain/amplification is a common copy number alteration in high-grade and metastatic prostate cancer. Associated with castration resistance and aggressive disease. Not directly targetable with currently approved agents.

5. AR (Xq12) — NOT AMPLIFIED
   - AR copy number within normal range; no AR amplification detected at this time.
   - Note: AR amplification is a common mechanism of acquired castration resistance and is not expected at this treatment-naive, castration-sensitive stage.

6. CDK4/CDK6 — NOT AMPLIFIED
7. EGFR — NOT AMPLIFIED
8. CCND1 — NOT AMPLIFIED
9. FGFR1/2 — NOT AMPLIFIED

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GENOMIC REARRANGEMENTS / FUSIONS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

- TMPRSS2-ERG fusion: NOT DETECTED
  (Consistent with ERG-negative status by IHC on diagnostic biopsy)
- ETV1, ETV4, ETV5 fusions: NOT DETECTED
- NTRK1/2/3 fusions: NOT DETECTED
- ALK rearrangements: NOT DETECTED
- RET rearrangements: NOT DETECTED
- RAF1 rearrangements: NOT DETECTED
- No other clinically significant fusions detected.

================================================================================
BIOMARKERS
================================================================================

Microsatellite Instability (MSI): MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
   - 46 microsatellite loci evaluated; 0 unstable loci
   - MSS status does not support pembrolizumab (Keytruda) eligibility based on MSI-H biomarker alone

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 4.2 mutations/Mb — LOW
   - Threshold for TMB-High: ≥10 mutations/Mb
   - TMB-Low status: does not meet FDA-approved biomarker threshold for TMB-directed pembrolizumab use

HRR Gene Status Summary:
   - BRCA2: BIALLELIC INACTIVATION (germline + LOH) — PATHOGENIC, clinically actionable
   - BRCA1: Wild-type
   - ATM: Wild-type
   - CDK12: Pathogenic frameshift (somatic) — HRR-associated, subclonal relative to BRCA2
   - PALB2: Wild-type
   - RAD51B/C/D: Wild-type
   - FANCA, FANCC, FANCL: Wild-type
   - CHEK2: Wild-type
   - Overall HRR classification: POSITIVE (BRCA2 biallelic inactivation present)

================================================================================
GERMLINE ANALYSIS — PERIPHERAL BLOOD
================================================================================
Germline Specimen: Peripheral blood (EDTA), collected concurrently with tissue submission.
Germline Platform: FoundationOne Germline validated germline panel.

GERMLINE PATHOGENIC VARIANT IDENTIFIED:

BRCA2 p.E1308* (c.3921G>A)
   - Germline status: PATHOGENIC GERMLINE VARIANT — CONFIRMED HETEROZYGOUS
   - ACMG Classification: Pathogenic (Class 5)
   - ClinVar: Classified Pathogenic; multiple submitters with concordant pathogenic classification
   - Tumor: Biallelic loss present (germline allele + somatic LOH) — consistent with classical Knudson two-hit model
   - Inheritance: Autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance; markedly elevated lifetime risk of prostate cancer (particularly high-grade), as well as elevated risk for breast, pancreatic, and ovarian cancers in biological relatives
   - Penetrance implications: BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant carriers have an estimated 8–12% lifetime risk of prostate cancer, with substantially elevated risk for lethal, metastatic disease. Association with early-onset and aggressive prostate cancer well-established.
   - Relevance to blood relatives: First-degree and second-degree relatives are at risk for harboring this germline variant. Familial testing should be offered through genetic counseling.
   - Therapeutic implications: BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant confers eligibility for PARP inhibitor therapy (olaparib per PROfound trial; rucaparib per TRITON2/TRITON3) in the setting of castration-resistant prostate cancer. Platinum-based chemotherapy sensitivity also supported.

NO OTHER CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT GERMLINE PATHOGENIC OR LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANTS DETECTED in:
   BRCA1, ATM, PALB2, CHEK2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, CDH1, TP53 (germline), APC, MUTYH, PTEN (germline), RAD51C, RAD51D.

GENETIC COUNSELING REFERRAL: STRONGLY RECOMMENDED
A referral to a certified genetic counselor has been placed. Patient and at-risk family members should be offered formal genetic counseling and cascade testing for the BRCA2 p.E1308* germline variant.

================================================================================
SUMMARY OF CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE FINDINGS
================================================================================

1. BRCA2 BIALLELIC INACTIVATION (germline p.E1308* + somatic LOH)
   - FDA-approved indication: Olaparib (Lynparza) and rucaparib (Rubraca) are approved for mCRPC with HRR gene alterations including BRCA2. At present (newly diagnosed mCSPC), PARP inhibitor therapy is not indicated; however, this alteration establishes eligibility for PARP inhibitor use upon development of castration resistance.
   - Platinum sensitivity: BRCA2-mutated tumors demonstrate enhanced sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin, cisplatin).

2. PTEN HOMOZYGOUS DELETION
   - Activates PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis. Investigational combinations targeting this pathway (e.g., ipatasertib + abiraterone per IPATential150 trial data in PTEN-loss patients) may be relevant. Not FDA-approved as standard of care at this time but may warrant clinical trial consideration.

3. CDK12 LOSS OF FUNCTION
   - CDK12-mutant tumors exhibit focal tandem duplications and elevated neoantigen burden; some patients may derive benefit from immunotherapy-based combinations (investigational). Current data insufficient to change management outside of clinical trial.

4. RB1 LOSS + TP53 MUTATION
   - Dual RB1/TP53 genomic lesions: high-risk signature for development of treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC). Baseline and on-treatment monitoring for NEPC features (neuroendocrine markers, symptom evolution, biopsy) recommended as disease progresses.

5. AR: WILD-TYPE, NOT AMPLIFIED
   - No current AR amplification or ligand-binding domain mutations at this castration-sensitive stage; reassessment recommended at time of castration resistance.

6. MSI-STABLE, TMB-LOW
   - Immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy (pembrolizumab) not supported by tumor genomic biomarkers at this time.

================================================================================
QUALITY METRICS
================================================================================
Median exon coverage: 847x
Percent exon bases ≥100x coverage: 99.1%
Specimen adequacy: Adequate (tumor nuclei 70%, exceeds 20% minimum threshold)
Sequencing QC: All quality metrics within acceptable parameters.
Note: Results are derived from tumor tissue (FFPE); sensitivity for detection of low-VAF variants may be limited. Results should be interpreted in the context of clinical history and other laboratory findings.

================================================================================
INTERPRETATION NOTES
================================================================================
This specimen demonstrates a complex, high-risk genomic profile consistent with de novo metastatic, high-grade prostate adenocarcinoma. The co-occurrence of BRCA2 biallelic loss (germline + LOH), TP53 hotspot mutation, PTEN homozygous deletion, and RB1 loss represents a convergence of multiple tumor suppressor defects associated with aggressive clinical behavior, castration resistance, and risk of neuroendocrine transdifferentiation. The SPOP p.F133V mutation is subclonal (VAF 14%), suggesting it may represent a secondary clonal event, and is notable in the context of a TMPRSS2-ERG fusion-negative tumor (consistent with the established SPOP/ERG mutual exclusivity in prostate cancer molecular taxonomy).

The confirmed BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant (p.E1308*) is of critical importance for patient management and hereditary cancer risk counseling for the patient and family members.

This report has been reviewed and signed by:
[Molecular Pathologist, MD, PhD — Foundation Medicine]
Report finalized: [see date of issue on file]

================================================================================
DISCLAIMER
================================================================================
This test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Foundation Medicine, Inc. It has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. This test is used for clinical purposes. It should not be regarded as investigational or for research. CLIA 22D2027531.
